title: Dog Vomiting Coffee Ground Texture: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog's vomit looks like coffee grounds, it indicates internal bleeding. This is a veterinary emergency. Learn what to do and when to seek help.
content_html:
TL;DR: Coffee ground vomit in dogs indicates internal bleeding and is a life-threatening emergency. You must transport your dog to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately to prevent shock or organ failure.

What does it mean if my dog's vomit looks like coffee grounds?

When a dog's vomit resembles dark, gritty coffee grounds, it is a clinical sign known as hematemesis. This texture is caused by blood that has been partially digested by stomach acid. The acid oxidizes the iron in the blood, turning it dark brown or black and creating a granular appearance. This indicates that bleeding is occurring somewhere in the upper gastrointestinal tract, such as the stomach or the beginning of the small intestine.

Is coffee ground vomit a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • Coffee ground vomit is a high-urgency medical emergency indicating internal bleeding that cannot be managed at home.
  • Potential causes include stomach ulcers, ingestion of toxins (such as rodenticides or human NSAIDs), severe infections, or gastrointestinal obstructions.
  • Internal bleeding can rapidly lead to shock or organ failure.
  • You should transport your dog to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately for professional intervention.

How does taking a photo of the vomit help the veterinarian with triage?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of the vomit before cleaning it up.
  • A photo provides the veterinary team with critical information regarding the volume of blood lost and the level of digestion that has occurred.
  • Visual evidence allows the triage nurse to more accurately assess the severity of your dog's condition.
  • The image may help the veterinarian narrow down the potential cause of the bleeding more quickly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, vomitus that has the appearance of coffee grounds suggests a bleeding lesion in the stomach, such as a gastric ulcer or neoplasm. The presence of hematemesis or melena warrants further investigation if clinical signs do not resolve within 2 days of symptomatic therapy, if the animal is systemically unwell, or if abnormalities are noted on abdominal palpation. Diagnostic testing, including a complete blood count, serum biochemical profile, urinalysis, and diagnostic imaging, may be necessary to determine the underlying cause.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 156)

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because the presence of digested blood indicates an active or recent internal hemorrhage, your dog needs immediate stabilization and diagnostic testing to prevent the condition from becoming life-threatening.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms I should look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for pale gums, extreme lethargy, a racing heart rate, or black, tarry stools (melena). These are all signs of significant blood loss and worsening shock.

faqs.question: Should I withhold food or water?
faqs.answer: Do not give your dog anything by mouth, as this could trigger more vomiting or interfere with emergency surgery if it is required. Head straight to the clinic.
